name: Ty Masterson
occupation: Kansas Senate President; Executive Director of GoCreate at Wichita State University
family: Married with family; they attend NewSpring Church together in Wichita
whoTheyAre:
Ty Masterson is the current President of the Kansas Senate and one of the most powerful Republicans in state government. He has represented Senate District 16 since 2009 and before that served on the Andover City Council. Outside the Legislature, he has worked as the executive director of GoCreate at Wichita State University, a maker space and innovation hub. He was born and raised in Butler County, Kansas.

Masterson and his family attend NewSpring Church in Wichita, a contemporary non-denominational church. His campaign biography says he chose to raise his family in Kansas because of safe communities, a low cost of living, and the chance to achieve the American Dream. He describes himself as a follower of Jesus Christ and says his core values are to honor your word, live your faith, and always put the people you serve first.

He is running for governor in the 2026 Republican primary against a crowded field that includes former Governor Jeff Colyer, Secretary of State Scott Schwab, and Insurance Commissioner Vicki Schmidt, among others.
theirRecord:
As Senate President, Masterson has been the single most influential person in the Kansas Legislature. He controls the legislative calendar, committee assignments, and which bills get a hearing. His campaign says he championed a $2 billion tax cut for Kansas families. He has led Republican priorities on taxes, education, elections, public safety, and social issues. He also pushed for changes to how Kansas selects Supreme Court justices, backing a constitutional amendment that would replace the current merit-based nominating commission with direct elections.

Masterson's power has also drawn criticism. When Insurance Commissioner Vicki Schmidt, a fellow Republican running against him for governor, championed a bill requiring insurance companies to cover breast cancer screening with no out-of-pocket costs, the bill missed key legislative deadlines and died. Democrats and some reporters said Masterson used his control over the Senate calendar to kill the bill as political retaliation against a primary opponent. A Masterson spokesman denied politics played any role, blaming pushback from the business community over mandated coverage costs. At the January 2026 Republican debate, when another candidate attacked him and others aggressively, Masterson pushed back by saying internal Republican fighting is what costs the party elections. He also led a delegation of Kansas lawmakers to Israel, saying it was important to understand the truth and spread it.
whatYouShouldKnow: Masterson is the Kansas Senate President, making him already one of the most powerful people in state government. He is not a newcomer; he has been setting the legislative agenda for years.
He has served in the Kansas Senate since 2009 and previously served on the Andover City Council.
His campaign says he championed a $2 billion tax cut for Kansas families during his time leading the Senate.

A breast cancer screening bill championed by his Republican primary opponent, Insurance Commissioner Vicki Schmidt, died in the Senate under his leadership. Democrats say he killed it for political reasons. His office says business community pushback was to blame.
His 2025 year-end campaign finance filing shows $695,998.16 raised, only $8,476.06 spent, and $687,522.10 cash on hand. The donor pattern is concentrated among a few large contributors.
He publicly identifies as a follower of Jesus Christ and attends NewSpring Church in Wichita with his family.
He led a Kansas legislative delegation visit to Israel and spoke publicly about the importance of that trip.
At the January 2026 GOP debate, he told a fellow candidate who was attacking others: 'This is not the enemy. The Democrats are the enemy.'
whereTheyWorship: Ty Masterson and his family attend NewSpring Church in Wichita, Kansas. NewSpring is a contemporary, non-denominational, evangelical church located at 12200 East 21st Street North in Wichita. Masterson's campaign biography confirms the family's attendance there, and he has publicly stated that he is a follower of Jesus Christ. His faith appears to directly inform his legislative work, particularly on life issues, where he has said that every life is precious and created by God.

church.name: NewSpring Church
church.details: Contemporary evangelical church in Wichita, Kansas. Masterson's campaign biography confirms that he and his family attend NewSpring Church. Located at 12200 East 21st Street North, Wichita, KS.

quotes.text: Born and raised in Butler County, Ty learned Kansas values early - live your faith, stand by your family, work hard, and if you're not willing to do something about it, don't waste time complaining.
quotes.source: Ty Masterson for Governor campaign website

quotes.text: I'm running for governor to take back Kansas from politicians like Laura Kelly who have hiked our taxes, padded the woke agenda, and left our schools and communities behind. Let's get to work.
quotes.source: Ty Masterson for Governor campaign website

quotes.text: I'm a follower of Jesus Christ.
Every life is precious -- it doesn't matter your background, your ethnicity, or your color... we're all one people, created by God.
That type of thing is why we lose in the general election. We spend time cutting each other off at the legs... This is not the enemy. The Democrats are the enemy.
Kansas isn't for sale, and we're taking it back.
quotes.source: Ty Masterson for Governor campaign website

campaignFinance.narrative: Masterson's 2025 year-end filing shows nearly $696,000 raised with almost nothing spent, leaving him with $687,522 cash on hand heading into 2026. That is a large financial cushion for a Kansas governor's race. The donor pattern is top-heavy, with several contributors giving the maximum $4,000 allowed under Kansas law and relatively few small-dollar donations visible in the filing.
campaignFinance.undisclosed: The full itemized donor schedule was not exhaustively parsed. Additional contributors are listed in the Kansas Ethics Commission filing.
